name: Controlled scale-up of residential capacity based on data-driven KPIs
consequences: Expanding from 8 to 12 suites increases enrollment capacity and the probability of reaching minimum adjudicated events, while raising ongoing obligations for staffing, safety coverage, and data storage. The downstream implications include higher fixed costs and potential delays if recruitment lags, as well as heightened risk of operational bottlenecks if annotation throughput does not scale accordingly.
options: Commence a staggered expansion plan to 12 suites only after achieving predefined data quality and event capture targets across two consecutive cohorts.
Delay expansion until external safety and staffing audits confirm capacity to maintain safe night coverage and timely incident response.
Implement a modular renovation and staffing approach that allows incremental capacity increases without disrupting ongoing observation.
review: Core tension centers on whether to grow capacity before data quality and staffing stability are proven; risk is over-committing resources before the system proves sustainable. Weakness: the options do not explicitly address staff burnout, training throughput, or contingency coverage during scale-up.

name: Diversify recruitment channels and inclusion criteria
consequences: Broadening eligibility and channels increases potential participant pool and generalizability, but adds heterogeneity that complicates event-triage benchmarking and safety monitoring. It may extend recruitment timelines and require revised consent materials and ethical approvals.
options: Broaden eligibility to include ages 18–75 and participants with stable comorbidities managed under supervision, enabling wider phenotyping.
Introduce adaptive, structured pre-admission screening with collateral history to refine candidate selection and improve event yield predictions.
Narrow recruitment to clearly defined NREM parasomnia subtypes with risk-based prioritization for high-yield phenotypes to accelerate method validation.
review: Core tension: representativeness versus homogeneous study cohorts; Weakness: options miss explicit safety oversight for comorbidities and potential regulatory concerns for broadened age range.

name: Strengthen algorithmic triage within the data workflow
consequences: Implementation reduces manual review burden and speeds scoring, but initial accuracy thresholds must be defined and monitored; mis-triage could bias event characterization and require iterative calibration. Downstream, improved efficiency supports larger datasets but demands robust validation to preserve scoring integrity.
options: Develop and deploy a real-time pre-scoring layer that stratifies nights by parasomnia likelihood, reducing manual review workload.
Integrate rule-based artifact rejection and cross-validation across nights to improve inter-rater reliability and annotation consistency.
Pilot a targeted post-hoc review cycle focusing on episodes with high uncertainty to preserve accuracy while keeping reviewer hours manageable.
review: Core tension: reliance on automated triage versus human gold standard; Weakness: options miss explicit thresholds for automated override and validation across diverse datasets.

name: Enhance privacy-safe data governance and cross-site validation
consequences: Strengthening governance improves reproducibility and external validation, but privacy constraints and GDPR compliance may slow data sharing and require legal overhead; cross-site validation will demand standardized protocols and data formats. These factors influence timelines and collaboration dynamics.
options: Establish formal data use agreements with external sleep centers to enable cross-site validation of event-triage models.
Pilot a European-wide de-identified physiological dataset consortium while maintaining strict privacy controls.
Limit data sharing to de-identified physiological streams and preserve video privacy with on-site analysis and non-deposited data.
review: Core tension: openness versus privacy; Weakness: options miss concrete data governance processes and consent management across jurisdictions.

name: Diversify funding and partnerships to build resilience
consequences: Diversification reduces reliance on a single funder, but EU and philanthropic funding cycles require compliance and longer lead times; industry partnerships may impose publication or data-access constraints. The outcome is greater financial stability with added governance obligations.
options: Pursue EU Framework Programme calls and national science funding to stabilize long-term support.
Cultivate philanthropic grants with clearly defined milestones and non-diagnostic data-sharing terms.
Negotiate industry collaborations focusing on non-core components with robust publication rights and transparent conflict-management.
review: Core tension: financial stability versus academic independence; Weakness: options miss explicit IP management, publication rights clarity, and conflict-of-interest governance.

name: Strengthen governance and risk management with external oversight
consequences: External oversight improves accountability and safety; however, frequent reviews may slow decision-making and create administrative overhead, potentially delaying actionable milestones. Clear escalation paths help maintain momentum but require careful design to avoid gridlock.
options: Institutionalize quarterly external reviews with predefined corrective actions and explicit time-bound milestones.
Publish interim safety and data-quality dashboards to the scientific advisory board and ethics committees.
Establish a risk-adjusted admission protocol with predefined triggers for recruitment criteria review and pause thresholds.
review: Core tension: governance speed versus rigor; Weakness: options miss explicit escalation pathways for deadlock or veto power and integration with the PI's operational authority.

name: Staff specialization and scheduling optimization for enhanced PSG capture
consequences: Direct effect: more consistent data capture and faster adjudication due to dedicated overnight technicians and standardized scoring. Downstream implication: increased personnel costs and staffing complexity, which may constrain expansion if budgets tighten; risk of scheduling bottlenecks during peak stay periods.
options: Hire and train a dedicated overnight sleep technologist team to conduct standardized PSG setups and real-time event capture across all active suites, ensuring consistent data quality.

Adopt overlapping two-tier night shift patterns to ensure rapid escalation during critical nights while preserving total headcount, though scheduling coordination becomes more complex and overtime exposure rises.
Contract external certified scorers to adjudicate a portion of overnight data on peak nights, maintaining data reliability without expanding on-site staff, while extending turnaround times.
review: Core tension: sustaining high-quality PSG capture while controlling labor costs and clinician burnout. Weakness: none of the options quantify budget impact or provide a clear path to scalable staffing under financial constraints.

name: Cross-institutional data-sharing for methodological benchmarking
consequences: Direct effect: external benchmarking improves validity of event-triage models and supports publications. Downstream: governance overhead and consent complexities, potential delays in sharing; privacy constraints may limit access to rich video data.
options: Draft formal data-sharing agreements with regional sleep centers to benchmark our event-triage algorithms against external datasets, strengthening methodological credibility and enabling joint publications.
Establish a small external advisory cohort to review data governance and analytic methods on a quarterly basis, accelerating transparency and reproducibility without compromising privacy.
Create a de-identified cross-site data repository with controlled access for approved researchers to reproduce analyses and enable external validation while preserving consent terms.
review: Core tension: external benchmarking vs privacy and consent; cross-site sharing could slow approvals. Weakness: options miss explicit governance for IP and publication rights; risk of incompatible data standards.

name: Adaptive recruitment and retention loops
consequences: Direct effect: improved enrollment efficiency and reduced dropouts; downstream: reliance on non-clinical engagement may increase cost; risk of selection bias if criteria adapt too aggressively.
options: Engage patient-advocate panels to co-design consent materials and study communications, reducing misunderstandings and improving retention across extended stays.
Implement flexible admission windows aligned with facility capacity and staff availability to optimize enrollment without compromising safety and ethics.
Pilot in-person pre-screening assessments combined with structured collateral-history collection to accelerate eligibility decisions while maintaining appropriate safeguards.
review: Core tension: recruitment velocity vs representativeness; adaptivity may introduce selection bias. Weakness: options don’t specify how to monitor and mitigate bias or ensure consent clarity.

name: Privacy-preserving data handling and analysis fidelity
consequences: Direct effect: strengthens data protection for sensitive video and sensor data; downstream: introduces additional technical controls and potential data processing delays, which may affect turnaround times.
options: Implement differential privacy-inspired perturbation for early analytics to protect identifiable information while preserving key phenotypic signals.
Develop synthetic event catalogs that mirror observed parasomnia features to validate triage tools without exposing real patient video or raw data.
Enforce strict audit trails and role-based access controls across all sensor streams and storage systems to guarantee traceability and regulatory compliance.
review: Core tension: privacy risk vs analytic utility; synthetic data may skew signaling if not careful. Weakness: options do not specify validation of synthetic data against real-world results or audit for re-identification risk.

name: Phase-gated expansion to 12 suites
consequences: Direct effect: ensures safety and quality before scale; downstream: may slow progress and require interim budget reallocation; risk of opportunity cost if milestones are not achieved.
options: Institute a formal phase gate that approves expansion to 12 suites only after achieving predefined data-quality benchmarks and safety indicators in the pilot phase.
Schedule external mid-project reviews to validate progress and adjust scope, funding, and staffing before committing to further capacity.
Develop a financial plan with reserve contingencies that align expansion pace with evidence of usable annotation throughput and acceptable false-alarm rates.
review: Core tension: expansion pace vs quality; expensive pilots could delay outputs. Weakness: options do not provide milestone-specific success metrics or contingency triggers.

name: Integrated regional partnerships for recruitment and follow-up
consequences: Direct effect: expands recruitment channels through regional clinics and neurologists, increasing eligible participants; downstream: governance and data-sharing coordination overhead across sites, potential delays.
options: Formalize multi-site recruitment agreements with regional sleep clinics and neurologists to streamline referrals and ensure consistent pre-admission screening.
Coordinate with insurers to streamline pre-admission coverage checks and consent processes, reducing delay and improving participant experience.
Offer travel stipends and meals to reduce participant burden and improve retention across extended residential monitoring periods.
review: Core tension: regional partnerships vs governance complexity; binding agreements may limit flexibility. Weakness: options miss explicit data-sharing boundaries and patient-privacy alignment across sites.

name: Staged admission windows aligned with staffing cycles
consequences: Adopting staged admissions reduces peak overnight coverage demands and improves safety oversight. A longer recruitment horizon can improve data quality by avoiding rushed annotation sessions; however, it may slow overall enrollment and extend project duration.
options: Stage admissions in waves that align with night-shift staffing plans to maintain safe coverage and steady annotation throughput.
Concentrate admissions during identified low-activity periods to minimize bottlenecks in scoring workflow and incident response.
Distribute admissions across pre-defined windows to dampen idle capacity and reduce peak burden while preserving enrollment momentum.
review: Core tension: balancing fast enrollment with consistent data maturation; weakness: the options do not specify contingency for slower event yield or regulatory delays that can derail timeline.

name: Semi-automated event triage to accelerate scoring
consequences: Implementing semi-automated triage reduces manual review time and speeds data throughput. Calibration and ongoing monitoring are necessary to avoid misclassification and bias, which may require periodic audits and threshold adjustments.
options: Implement semi-automated event triage that flags high-probability parasomnias for rapid review and reserves full PSG scoring for ambiguous cases.
Develop rule-based annotation cues integrated into technologist workflow to accelerate line-by-line scoring and reduce fatigue.
Schedule random audits ensuring inter-rater reliability while relying on automated triage for high-confidence events.
review: Core tension: reliance on automation may introduce systematic misses; weakness: the options do not specify performance targets, calibration cadence, or thresholds.

name: Video data governance with strict access controls
consequences: Establish privacy-centric governance with tiered access, audit logs, and on-site retention controls; downstream, this may constrain cross-site sharing and require governance overhead.
options: Implement strict access-controls with role-based permissions and regular cryptographic audits for video data.
Deploy local on-site encryption and secure data transfer pipelines to minimize exposure in transit.
Institute quarterly privacy reviews with external counsel to align with data protection standards.
review: Core tension: privacy vs research agility; weakness: options lack explicit de-identification processes and retention timelines.

name: Regional recruitment consortium within Bonn region
consequences: Formal partnerships broaden referrals and diversify recruitment, increasing catchment reach and enrollment pace; downstream, coordination complexity and shared governance requirements increase.
options: Establish formal partnerships with neighboring sleep clinics and neurologists to broaden referrals and share screening standards.
Create a participant ambassador program to improve retention and adherence with peer support throughout stays.
Coordinate joint information sessions across Bonn institutions to standardize pre-admission pathways and consent processes.
review: Core tension: external partnerships risk protocol consistency; weakness: lacks explicit data-sharing governance and consent alignment across sites.

name: Adaptive staffing model linked to event-yield forecasts
consequences: Tailor night-shift staffing to anticipated yield, reducing idle time and improving safety coverage; downstream, forecasting errors increase risk and cost volatility.
options: Use live monitoring of event yield to dynamically adjust night-shift staffing budgets and assignments.
Pre-arrange on-call rosters with flexible hours to cover peak nights while preserving worker wellbeing.
Institute a rotating duty cycle that distributes high-load nights evenly to reduce fatigue and burnout.
review: Core tension: dependence on yield forecasts vs real-time variability; weakness: lacks contingency plan for forecast error.

name: Data-sharing and publication governance for reproducibility
consequences: Codify data-use agreements and publish de-identified datasets and methods to enable external validation; downstream, this can attract scrutiny and require additional approvals.
options: Create an internal data governance charter that codifies data usage, sharing, and retention with quarterly reviews.
Publish de-identified datasets and methods protocols in aligned repositories to accelerate reproducibility while preserving privacy.
Archive governance changes with version control and stakeholder sign-off to ensure traceability.
review: Core tension: openness vs privacy and competitive advantage; weakness: options do not specify consent processes or data-use restrictions from participants.
